coredata.dc:description: Halide perovskite solar cells (HPSCs) are a promising technology for solar energy conversion, owing to their low cost and high efficiency. However, their practical deployment is limited by instability and degradation under prolonged sunlight exposure. This study addresses these challenges by integrating fluorescent solar concentrators (FSCs) with HPSCs. FSCs, which combine fluorescent materials with an optical waveguide medium, offer a promising solution by enhancing light harvesting while reducing the direct exposure of solar cells. We present a multi-colored carbon dots (CDs)-based FSC designed to tackle key issues related to light efficiency, reabsorption, and stability. The CDs are synthesized to exhibit high photoluminescence quantum yield, substantial Stokes shifts, and excellent photothermal stability. Additionally, the optical waveguide structure is optimized through simulation tools to maximize light gathering efficiency. Simulation results demonstrate that a five-layered equilateral triangular waveguide structure can achieve up to 100% light gathering efficiency, effectively overcoming the limitations of traditional materials’ refractive indices. The integration of optimized FSCs with HPSCs reduces temperature stress, mitigates UV degradation, and minimizes spatial footprint. The combination of the multi-colored CDs and optimized waveguide structure leads to a significant performance boost, achieving an optical efficiency of up to 16.95%. This work offers a cost-effective and scalable approach for improving the optical efficiency and stability of HPSCs, with promising applications in building-integrated photovoltaics, smart windows, and rooftop solar systems.

head.ce:title: Material design and optical waveguide model development for fluorescent concentrator devices to improve the performance of perovskite solar cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Halide perovskite solar cells (HPSCs) are a promising technology for solar energy conversion, owing to their low cost and high efficiency. However, their practical deployment is limited by instability and degradation under prolonged sunlight exposure. This study addresses these challenges by integrating fluorescent solar concentrators (FSCs) with HPSCs. FSCs, which combine fluorescent materials with an optical waveguide medium, offer a promising solution by enhancing light harvesting while reducing the direct exposure of solar cells. We present a multi-colored carbon dots (CDs)-based FSC designed to tackle key issues related to light efficiency, reabsorption, and stability. The CDs are synthesized to exhibit high photoluminescence quantum yield, substantial Stokes shifts, and excellent photothermal stability. Additionally, the optical waveguide structure is optimized through simulation tools to maximize light gathering efficiency. Simulation results demonstrate that a five-layered equilateral triangular waveguide structure can achieve up to 100% light gathering efficiency, effectively overcoming the limitations of traditional materials’ refractive indices. The integration of optimized FSCs with HPSCs reduces temperature stress, mitigates UV degradation, and minimizes spatial footprint. The combination of the multi-colored CDs and optimized waveguide structure leads to a significant performance boost, achieving an optical efficiency of up to 16.95%. This work offers a cost-effective and scalable approach for improving the optical efficiency and stability of HPSCs, with promising applications in building-integrated photovoltaics, smart windows, and rooftop solar systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Halide perovskite solar cells (HPSCs) have emerged as one of the most promising third-generation photovoltaic technologies due to their low-cost, solution-based processing, simplified manufacturing, and high energy conversion efficiency [1–3]. These advantages position HPSCs for transformative advancements in the solar cell industry. However, under practical operating conditions, prolonged sunlight exposure leads to significant degradation in their photovoltaic performance. This degradation is primarily attributed to the inherent instability of halide perovskite materials, which arise from their “soft” ionic nature and dynamic surface equilibrium [4,5]. These characteristics make them highly susceptible to rapid decomposition under intense ultraviolet (UV) irradiation and elevated temperature conditions [6,7]. Therefore, enhancing the photothermal stability and long-term performance of HPSCs—particularly their durability under environmental conditions—remains a critical challenge for their commercialization.

Fluorescent solar concentrators (FSCs) are optical devices that integrate fluorescent conversion materials with optical waveguide medium. The FSC system typically consists of high-fluorescence-efficiency conversion materials, a transparent optical waveguide, and photovoltaic cells (PVCs) [8]. The device operates through three main steps: light absorption, fluorescence emission, and light transmission. When sunlight enters the waveguide, the fluorescent materials absorb the light, become excited, and emit longer-wavelength fluorescence. This fluorescence is then guided within the waveguide through total internal reflection, ultimately being absorbed by PVCs coupled to the optical waveguide, thus enabling efficient conversion of light energy into electricity [9–11]. Integrating FSCs with HPSCs addresses several challenges, including photothermal stability and scalable production, while significantly reducing costs and conserving space. This approach holds great promise for applications in building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV), smart windows, and rooftop photovoltaics [12–14]. However, the main bottlenecks to high-performance FSCs remain the efficiency and stability of fluorescent materials, spectral matching between the emission spectrum of the fluorescent material and the bandgap of the solar cell, and the optimization of the optical waveguide structure [15].

The selection and performance of fluorescent materials directly influence the light conversion efficiency of FSCs. Commonly used materials include quantum dots, rare-earth complexes, and organic dye molecules [16–19]. To achieve high-performance FSCs, these materials must exhibit a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), large Stokes shift, broad absorption spectrum, excellent photothermal stability, and emission wavelengths that align with the bandgap of the photovoltaic cells [20–22]. Among these materials, carbon dots (CDs) stand out due to their tunable absorption properties, high PLQY, excellent photothermal stability, low toxicity, and the variety of precursors available for synthesis [23–27]. For instance, Zhao et al. developed a simple and controllable vacuum heating method to synthesize CDs with an exceptionally PLQY and a large Stokes shift [28,29]. These properties make the CDs particularly well-suited for FSC applications, where high efficiency and broad emission characteristics are critical. Moreover, CDs can effectively match the bandgap of PVCs, making them an attractive candidate for FSCs. Consequently, CDs have garnered increasing attention in recent years. Current research on CD-based FSCs focuses on improving efficiency, stability, and scalability to meet practical application requirements [30–32]. However, the light conversion efficiencies reported for CD-based FSCs remain relatively low, limiting their potential in applications such as BIPV, smart windows, and rooftop photovoltaics. Therefore, the development of multi-color, highly efficient CDs suitable for large-area FSCs is crucial for improving FSC performance, reducing fabrication costs, and bridging the gap between theoretical and practical applications.

FSCs rely primarily on the principle of total internal reflection (TIR) to concentrate and transmit light. The light gathering efficiency of the FSC is largely determined by the refractive index and geometric structure of the optical waveguide material. According to TIR principles, optical waveguide materials are typically chosen from high-refractive-index materials (e.g., glass, PMMA, and PVP, with refractive indices around 1.5) to enhance light concentration [33]. However, even when high-refractive-index waveguide materials are used (with a refractive index of 1.5 and a critical angle for TIR of 41.8°), theoretical calculations under ideal conditions show that only approximately 74.5 % of the photons emitted by the fluorescent material can enter the waveguide mode, with the remaining photons inevitably escaping the concentrator [13,34,35]. When photons undergo refraction or reflection at the interfaces of optical waveguides with different geometries, it is possible to redesign the waveguide structure to redirect escaping photons back into the waveguide, where they can eventually be absorbed by the PVCs. By carefully optimizing the geometric design of the waveguide, the limitations imposed by the material’s refractive index can be overcome, significantly enhancing the concentrator's light concentration efficiency.

To address these challenges, we have systematically optimized the design of both fluorescent conversion materials and optical waveguide media for FSCs. Initially, we synthesized multi-colored CDs using an ultrasonic microreaction method and performed comprehensive characterization of their crystalline phases and morphological structures. Three representative CDs with distinct luminescent properties were selected for further analysis, focusing on their optical performance, including fluorescence emission spectra, excitation-emission behaviors, Stokes shift, PLQY, and photothermal stability. After optimization, the synthesized CDs demonstrated several remarkable advantages, including high PLQY (≥72 %), substantial Stokes shift (≥1.13 eV), excellent photothermal stability, and low production costs. To enhance the light gathering efficiency of the optical waveguide medium, we developed a structural model of the optical waveguide using multiphysics simulation software and employed ray tracing techniques to assess the impact of waveguide geometry, layer count, and varying fluorescence incident angles on concentrator efficiency. The optimization results revealed that a five-layered equilateral triangular waveguide structure allowed the fluorescence incident angle to vary from 0° to 90°, surpassing the traditional critical incident angle limit of 41.8°. Moreover, the optimized FSCs achieved an efficiency exceeding 80 % across a wide range of incident angles, with a peak efficiency of 100 % (incident angle φ = 45°). By integrating the optimized CDs and optical waveguide medium with HPSCs, we effectively prevented direct sunlight exposure, reduced UV radiation, and lowered the operating temperature of the devices. Additionally, we significantly reduced the required active area of the HPSCs, leading to cost savings and a smaller spatial footprint. This optimization strategy enhanced the light gathering efficiency, improving the overall photovoltaic conversion efficiency of the HPSCs and further boosting their long-term performance and reliability.

Aqueous ammonium citrate (A.R.), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8 %), petroleum ether (PE, A.R.), and poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, Mw ∼ 35000) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, P.R. China). The perovskite solar cells (PSCs) were purchased from the Lingguang Electronic Products Business Department, Donghu New Technology Development Zone (Wuhan, P.R. China). All chemicals were used as received, without further purification. Deionized water (H2O, 18.2 MΩ·cm) was employed for all experimental procedures.

The CDs were synthesized using an ultrasonic microreactor, following the method previously reported [24–26]. Anhydrous ammonium citrate was employed as the precursor, DMF as the modifier, and water as the solvent. In the first step, anhydrous ammonium citrate was dissolved in water to form precursor A, while an equal volume of DMF was used to prepare precursor B. Prior to injection into the microreactor, the ultrasonic frequency was calibrated, and the temperature control unit was activated. Once the target temperature was reached, both the high-precision syringe pump and the ultrasonic control unit were simultaneously initiated to ensure the cleaning of the microchannel. Subsequently, the supply voltage to the temperature control unit was adjusted to maintain a stable temperature within the microreactor, facilitating continuous fluid flow.

Precursors A and B were then introduced into two high-precision syringe pumps, where their flow rates were carefully regulated to ensure stable and consistent injection into the microreactor. Under the combined effects of ultrasonic cavitation, elevated temperature, and controlled pressure, the precursor solutions underwent rapid condensation and carbonization reactions, resulting in the formation of CDs with a high fluorescence yield and superior quality. After synthesis, the products were purified by precipitation with PE three times to remove any residual impurities. The purified CDs were then dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1,000 Da to eliminate unreacted precursors. The final product was collected in a vial and stored in ice-cold water for subsequent use. By adjusting the volume ratio of water and DMF in the precursor mixture, three distinct types of CDs were obtained: blue-emitting (B-CDs), green-emitting (G-CDs), and red-emitting (R-CDs), each exhibiting unique fluorescence characteristics.

The FSC consists of a stacked structure, including an optical waveguide medium layer, a fluorescent material conversion layer, and an ultra-clear glass layer, as shown in Fig. 1 . It also incorporates a perovskite solar cell module and copper wires, positioned on the side of the optical waveguide medium layer. The fluorescent material conversion layer is composed of fluorescent carbon dot materials and polymers, while the optical waveguide medium layer is made of highly transparent PMMA.

All four side faces of the optical waveguide medium layer can be equipped with HPSC modules. The side faces not featuring perovskite solar cell modules are mirror-coated to ensure that any randomly emitted fluorescence undergoes specular reflection on the mirror surfaces. This effectively prevents fluorescence escape and maximizes fluorescence absorption by the HPSC modules. Similarly, the bottom of the Ultra-clear glass is also mirror-coated, further minimizing fluorescence escape and promoting absorption by the perovskite solar cells, thereby enhancing the light-gathering efficiency and improving photoelectric conversion.

The structural design of the optical waveguide dielectric structure was simulated using COMSOL Multiphysics 6.1 (COMSOL, Sweden). The crystalline phase of the product was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8-ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) with a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15418 nm) at 35 kV and a scan rate of 2°/min, over a range of 5° to 60°. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) was performed at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a UV–Vis spectrometer (TU-1901, Purkinje, China). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the product was recorded using a xenon lamp-based fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan). The PLQY of the CDs was determined according to the following procedure [36]:

where the subscript “X” and “st” refers to the integrated fluorescence intensity, A to the absorption intensity at 365 nm, n to the refractive index of the solvent, and the subscripts X and st denote the sample and reference, respectively. Quinine sulfate (PLQY = 0.54) was used as the reference.

The photovoltaic performance of the fabricated FSCs was evaluated under simulated solar illumination using a Solar Simulator CEL-AAAS100 (Beijing Zhongjiao Jinyuan Technology Co., Ltd.), equipped with a standard AM1.5G filter and a 100 W xenon arc lamp integrated with a reflective housing. The light intensity was precisely calibrated to 100 mW∙cm−2 using an optical power meter to ensure standardized testing conditions. For both power conversion efficiency (PCE) and incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) measurements, a photovoltaic cell was directly coupled to one edge of the FSC to collect the guided light. The PVCs employed in this study had physical dimensions of 5.0 × 5.0 cm2, with an active area of 3.8 × 3.8 cm2. To eliminate any interference from direct illumination and isolate the contribution of waveguided photons, all photosensitive areas of the PVCs not interfacing with the FSC edge were carefully masked using opaque tape.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, morphological, and optical characterizations of multicolor luminescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the structural and morphological characteristics of the as-synthesized CDs, XRD and TEM analyses were performed. The XRD patterns of the B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs exhibited distinct features characteristic of carbon-based materials, with prominent diffraction peaks, as shown in Fig. 2 a, 2d, and 3 g. Specifically, all three types of CDs displayed a single broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 29.2°, 29.6°, and 29.6°, respectively, corresponding to the (002) plane of a graphite-like carbon structure (JCPDS PDF #75-2078) [37]. These diffraction peaks are indicative of the presence of an ordered carbon structure with a nanoscale graphene domain. Importantly, no additional peaks associated with impurities were observed, further confirming the high purity of the synthesized CDs. This result strongly supports the successful formation of a nanoscale carbogenic core within the CDs.

To further explore the morphology and size distribution of the CDs, TEM imaging was conducted. As shown in Fig. 2b, 2e, and 2 h, the three types of CDs were found to be uniformly distributed with well-defined spherical shapes and narrow size distributions. The images clearly reveal that the particles are well-dispersed without significant aggregation, which is crucial for their optical properties. The corresponding size distribution histograms (Fig. 2c, 2f, and 2i) revealed average diameters of 3.18 ± 0.1 nm for B-CDs, 3.26 ± 0.1 nm for G-CDs, and 4.25 ± 0.1 nm for R-CDs. These results indicate that the CDs are small, monodisperse nanoparticles with consistent sizes, which is essential for ensuring stable fluorescence emission and high PLQY.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra, PLQY, and photothermal stability of the B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were systematically analyzed to comprehensively evaluate their optical properties. As depicted in Fig. 3 a–c, all three types of CDs exhibited distinct absorption features spanning from the ultraviolet to visible regions. In the high-energy region, all CDs demonstrated strong absorption, which can be attributed to the π–π* transitions of C=C bonds in the sp2 hybridized carbon core [38]. In the lower-energy region, however, their absorption characteristics diverged. Specifically, the B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs displayed absorption peaks at 324 nm, 335 nm, and 340 nm, respectively. These absorption bands are associated with the n–π* transitions of surface states containing C=N and C=O functional groups, which are known to contribute to the surface chemistry of CDs [39]. Notably, the G-CDs exhibited stronger absorption than both the B-CDs and R-CDs, suggesting a higher density of C=C, C=N, and C=O groups on the surface of the G-CDs [25]. This observation implies that the surface states of the CDs, in addition to the carbon core structure, plays a significant role in defining their optical properties. Despite these differences, the UV–Vis absorption profiles of the three CDs were similar, indicating that they were synthesized through analogous synthetic routes. The variations in peak intensities, however, reflect differences in the carbon core structure and the distribution of surface states among the samples.

The PL emission spectra of the CDs, as shown in Fig. 3a–c, revealed emission peaks at 460 nm, 525 nm, and 645 nm for the B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively, covering a broad range of the visible spectrum. This observation suggests that these CDs are capable of emitting light across a wide spectrum, making them suitable for various optoelectronic applications. The Stokes shifts were found to be greater than 1.13 eV for all three samples. A large Stokes shift is beneficial for reducing spectral overlap between absorption and emission, thereby minimizing self-absorption and energy transfer losses. This characteristic is crucial for enhancing PLQY, making these CDs highly promising for applications that require strong fluorescence and stable emission properties across the visible spectrum.

To further investigate the excitation-dependent PL characteristics of the CDs, normalized PL emission spectra were recorded under varying excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 3d–f. With increasing excitation wavelength, the maximum emission wavelengths of the B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs exhibited a significant redshift, indicative of excitation-dependent PL behavior. This redshift is consistent with that observed in other carbon dots, suggesting that the emission properties are governed by the heterogeneity of the emissive states, which are influenced by both the carbon core structure and the surface functionalization [40–42].

XPS was employed to characterize the chemical bonding and composition of the CDs, as shown in Fig. S1. The high-resolution XPS spectra reveal that the C1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks at approximately 284.6 eV, 286.2 eV, and 287.6 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbon (C=C/C–C), sp3 carbon (C–N/C–O), and carbonyl groups (C=O/C=N), respectively. The N1s spectra show three components at about 398.6 eV, 399.5 eV, and 400.2 eV, attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N. The O1s spectrum shows two components at approximately 531.3 eV and 532.8 eV, corresponding to C=O and C–O, respectively. A decrease in the blue shift of the emission wavelength was observed with a lower carbon concentration and reduced degree of carbonization, as determined by comparing the C content (see Table S1). Additionally, the O/C ratios of the R-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs were 0.80, 0.93, and 1.13, respectively, while the N/C ratios were 0.45, 0.57, and 0.49, respectively, indicating that the oxygen contents progressively increased from R-CDs to B-CDs. Generally, a higher-quality carbon skeleton enhances PLQY, while nitrogen doping improves electron migration and reduces recombination losses, typically resulting in higher PLQY. However, excessive oxidation can lead to non-radiative recombination, which may diminish PLQY. These findings are consistent with the observed trends in Fig. 3i.

Stability is a critical factor in assessing the practical applicability of fluorescent materials. In this regard, the thermal and photostability of the B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were rigorously evaluated under conditions of heat treatment and prolonged UV irradiation, as shown in Fig. 3g and 3 h. Upon heating the CDs at 80 °C for different durations, their fluorescence intensities gradually decreased over time, with the R-CDs exhibiting the most pronounced decline. After 120 h of heat exposure, the fluorescence intensities of the three CDs retained between 80 % and 87 % of their initial values (Fig. 3g). This indicates that the CDs maintain a significant portion of their fluorescence even after extended thermal stress. In addition, when subjected to continuous UV exposure at 365 nm for 90 h at room temperature, the PL intensities of the CDs remained at 76 %–86 % of their original values (Fig. 3h). These results highlight the high thermal and photostability of the CDs, demonstrating their robustness under both elevated temperature and UV light exposure. Such stability is essential for applications where long-term performance and reliability are critical.

To facilitate an intuitive comparison of the fluorescence efficiency, the PLQY of the CDs was measured, as shown in Fig. 3i. The PLQY values of the R-CDs (80 %) and G-CDs (84 %) were found to be higher than that of the B-CDs (72 %). High fluorescence efficiency is a key parameter for FSCs, as it directly impacts the efficiency of light-to-electricity conversion. Therefore, the high PLQY values of the G-CDs and R-CDs make them particularly suitable for use in FSCs, where efficient light harvesting and minimal energy losses are crucial for optimal photoelectric performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and numerical simulation of optical waveguide structure for the FSC
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of the optical waveguide medium for the FSC was carried out using modeling and simulation in COMSOL Multiphysics. A commonly used optical material was selected for the waveguide, with dimensions of 50 mm × 30 mm × 12.5 mm, which is typical for devices of this nature. Ray tracing simulations were performed within the geometric optics interface of COMSOL, enabling the analysis of light propagation through the waveguide. By investigating light paths corresponding to varying incident angles across different structural configurations, we optimized the waveguide design to maximize the light-gathering efficiency of the FSC.

In the COMSOL simulation, the optical waveguide model, with the same 50 × 30 × 12.5 mm dimensions, utilized a ray-tracing approach within the geometrical optics framework. The design included reflective surfaces on three sides—left, front, and back—to ensure efficient specular reflection of the emitted fluorescence. The waveguide structure was configured with the fluorescent material coated on the bottom surface, and the entire assembly was capped with reflective mirrors. The right side of the waveguide was coupled with the photovoltaic cell, while the top remained open to allow light to enter and exit (see Fig. 4 a and Fig. S2a). Fluorescence emitted from the coated bottom surface is absorbed by the photovoltaic cell on the right side, while a portion of the light exits through the top, contributing to the overall light-harvesting efficiency.

To simplify the analysis, the light propagation and absorption process was modeled in two dimensions (2D). This reduction to a 2D spatial dimension was implemented in COMSOL, with the waveguide medium modeled as a rectangular structure (50 mm × 12.5 mm) to represent the core functional area of the system (see Fig. 4b and Fig. S2b). The refractive index of the optical waveguide material was set to 1.5, a value representative of common materials such as glass, PMMA, or PVP. For the surrounding air, the refractive index was set to 1, completing the basic setup for the numerical simulation environment. This approach allows for efficient modeling of light interactions within the waveguide and helps to accurately predict the optical efficiency of the FSC system, guiding further optimization of the design parameters.

To quantify the light absorption by the HPSC and evaluate the light harvesting efficiency of the waveguide, we activated two light detectors positioned at the top and right regions of the waveguide model in COMSOL, while deactivating detectors in all other regions. This setup allowed for precise measurement of the light absorbed by the HPSC, as depicted in Fig. 4b and Fig. S2b. The refractive index of the right-side rectangular region was set to 1, with Light Detector B placed there to measure the number of rays converging toward the photovoltaic cell (denoted as N 1). Similarly, the rectangular region above the waveguide was assigned a refractive index of 1, and Light Detector A was used to track the rays escaping from the top surface (denoted as N 2). The total number of rays passing through the waveguide medium is the sum of those exiting through the top (N 2) and those converging toward the right side (N 1). The light-gathering efficiency, η, is defined as the ratio of the rays converging toward the photovoltaic cell (N 1) to the total number of rays passing through the waveguide medium, as given in Equation (2).
(2)
η =
N 1
N 1
+
N 2

The fluorescence emitted by the CDs upon excitation was assumed to radiate randomly in a semi-circular direction upwards from the bottom boundary of the waveguide, with the angle between the emitted light and the normal to the bottom boundary defined as the incident angle – φ (as shown in Fig. 4b, ranging from 0° to 90°). In COMSOL, a 1D plot group was configured, where the y-axis represents the η for light incident at a given angle, and the x-axis represents the fluorescence emission time.

These findings underscore the importance of the structural design and material selection of the optical waveguide medium in optimizing the performance of FSCs. The simulations not only provide insights into the fundamental principles governing light propagation and energy capture within the waveguide but also offer a pathway for further optimization to achieve higher light gathering efficiency in future FSC devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the bottom geometry of a single-layer optical waveguide structure
ce:section.ce:para: During the design and simulation of the optical waveguide structure, significant optical losses were identified at the heterogeneous interface between the bottom surface of the waveguide medium and the fluorescence layer. These losses primarily arose from the refractive index mismatch, which hindered efficient light coupling and transmission. To mitigate these losses and optimize the waveguide's performance, we performed simulations exploring various bottom geometries for the waveguide medium, incorporating multi-angle incident fluorescence.

As depicted in Fig. 5 a, we replaced the conventional flat-bottomed waveguide design with a wavy surface configuration. In this modified structure, fluorescence emitted from the lower layer traverses the undulating surface before entering the waveguide medium. The fluorescence layer was modeled to emit 100 uniformly distributed rays per emission event, with φ set to 30°, 41.8°, 45°, and 60°, as illustrated in Fig. 5b–e. The simulation results reveal that, compared to the flat-bottomed waveguide medium, the wavy-bottomed design facilitates total internal reflection of some light rays, which are then successfully guided into the right-side face of the waveguide when the φ is below 41.8°. However, for φ exceeding 41.8°, light within the rectangular waveguide undergoes complete internal reflection and remains confined within the medium. In contrast, due to the altered surface geometry in the wavy waveguide, some light refracts at the interface, causing a portion to escape through the upper surface of the waveguide medium.

To comprehensively evaluate the performance of the wavy optical waveguide, it is crucial to quantify its η at various φ. This will help determine whether the wavy design offers superior light collection capabilities compared to the conventional flat-bottomed configuration. The simulation results for η, presented in Fig. 5f–i, show a significant improvement over the flat-bottomed design. Specifically, the number of light rays received at the right-side face of the waveguide was substantially higher with the wavy surface. Furthermore, the η exhibited a marked increase as the incident angle became steeper, resulting in a η avg. of 67 %. Although this design improves the η avg., it also introduces greater complexity in the light propagation paths, which may complicate the development of an optimal pattern for light absorption. This trade-off between efficiency and complexity highlights the need for further refinement to achieve an ideal balance in light collection and waveguide design.

To further optimize the waveguide medium, we investigated an alternative modification to the bottom surface: a serrated structure. As depicted in Fig. S3a, this design incorporates a 45° serrated pattern on the bottom surface of the waveguide medium, with fluorescence emitted from the lower layer traversing the serrated surface before entering the waveguide. Simulations were conducted using 100 uniformly distributed rays, emitted at φ of 30°, 41.8°, 45°, and 60° (Figs. S3b–e). The resulting light gathering efficiencies are presented in Figs. S3f–i. The η avg. for this configuration reached 72 %. However, while the serrated structure alters the direction of the rays entering the waveguide, it did not substantially optimize the angles required for total internal reflection. Consequently, a significant portion of the emitted light escaped through the upper surface of the waveguide medium. This indicates that, although the serrated design represents a step forward, it does not contribute significantly to the overall optimization of light gathering efficiency for the concentrator.

Building on the light cone effect observed in PCVs, we propose a novel optical waveguide structure with an equilateral triangle base, as depicted in Fig. 6 a, Fig. S4, and Fig. S5. This design is intended to further enhance light confinement within the waveguide. In this configuration, the fluorescence layer emits 100 uniformly distributed rays per event, with incident angles of 0°, 30°, 41.8°, 45°, and 60° (Fig. 6b–e). Detailed simulations show that within the incident angle range of 40° to 50°, the symmetric triangular geometry of the optical waveguide effectively confines the fluorescence, minimizing its escape and significantly improving light retention within the waveguide. The corresponding concentration efficiencies for each incident angle are presented in Fig. 6f–i. When the φ is 45°, the η reaches its maximum value of 100 %. The η avg. for this optimized configuration was calculated to be 81.5 % across the 0° to 90° incident angle range, representing a substantial improvement over previous designs. This enhancement overcomes the limitations associated with the refractive index of the material. These results highlight that the equilateral triangle base design, combined with strategically placed serrated structures, offers a promising approach to increasing efficiency in FSCs. This optimized structure not only improves total internal reflection but also reduces light escape, making it a highly effective candidate for the next generation of high-performance FSCs.

However, it is worth noting that for φ outside the 40° to 50° range, while most of the fluorescence is concentrated on the right side of the waveguide, a significant portion of the light still escapes from the upper surface of the waveguide material. This observation suggests that the symmetric equilateral triangle structure effectively alters the φ and optimizes the η within this range. For incident angles that are more challenging to constrain, the escaping rays exhibit a clear and uniform distribution. To further enhance the η avg. of the optical waveguide, future optimization could involve adjusting the φ of the rays approaching the escape threshold. This modification would increase the number of rays undergoing total internal reflection, thereby further improving the overall light concentration and retention within the waveguide medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the bottom geometry of multi-layer optical waveguide structure
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the results from the light waveguide medium with an equilateral triangular structure, we observed that, under identical φ, even with the random distribution of fluorescence sources, the φ of the fluorescence entering the modified triangular waveguide structure remained unchanged. Consequently, the light rays escaping from the surface of the waveguide medium exhibited a distinct and predictable pattern. Within the waveguide, increasing the φ of the rays multiple times—achieved by employing a multilayer waveguide structure—enhanced the concentrator's ability to confine the fluorescence. Building on this observation, we further investigated the number of layers and stacking configurations of the optical waveguide medium, as shown in Figs. S6a and S6b.

We first investigated the impact of varying the number of layers on the η of a Type I parallel stacking configuration for the optical waveguide medium, as shown in Fig. S6a. Next, we examined the η for different φ when the waveguide medium was stacked in five layers with a Type I parallel configuration. The fluorescence layer emitted 100 uniformly distributed rays per emission event, with φ values set to 0°, 30°, 41.8°, 45°, 60°, and 75°, as depicted in Figs. S7b–g. Simulation results indicate that the Type I triangular structure with parallel stacking significantly improves fluorescence confinement for φ ≥ 45°. For fluorescence with larger φ, the light path undergoes multiple refractions and reflections, reducing light escape from the waveguide. For φ < 45°, while light escape is reduced, a significant portion still exits the medium. As a result, when φ = 45°, η reaches its maximum value of 100 %, and the overall ηavg. increases to 88.7 % across the 0° to 90° incident angle range.

Next, we investigated the effect of different layer numbers on the concentrator efficiency of the waveguide medium with alternating Type I and Type II stacking, as shown in Fig. S6b. Specifically, we focused on the concentrator efficiency for typical incident angles in the five-layer configuration with alternating Type I and Type II stacking. The fluorescence layer emitted 100 uniformly distributed rays per emission event, with φ set at 0°, 30°, 41.8°, 45°, 60°, and 75°, as illustrated in the simulation results in Fig. 7 b–g. Detailed simulation results reveal that for fluorescence φ greater than 45°, the alternating Type I and Type II triangular waveguide structure provides slightly lower fluorescence confinement compared to the Type I parallel stacking configuration (Fig. 7e–g). However, when compared to the single-layer Type I structure, the amount of light escaping from the waveguide medium is significantly reduced. For fluorescence with φ smaller than 45°, the alternating Type I and Type II stacking structure demonstrates improved fluorescence confinement compared to the Type I parallel stacking configuration (Fig. 7b–d).

Fig. 8 a-f show the η within 1 ns following fluorescence emission. The results indicate that by approximately 0.5 ns, the reflection and refraction processes within the optical waveguide medium are largely completed, and η gradually stabilizes. For all six incident angles (φ = 0°, 30°, 41.8°, 45°, 60°, and 75°), η exceeds 80 %, with a maximum of 100 % at φ = 45°. The overall ηavg. is calculated to be 89 %. These results suggest that, with five stacking layers, the final ηavg. remains nearly identical between the Type I parallel stacking and the alternating Type I and Type II stacking configurations.

To identify (Fig. 8) the optimal number of layers for the optical waveguide medium that maximizes concentrator efficiency, we systematically examined the η across different stacking configurations under various fluorescence φ as shown in Fig. 9 . Specifically, Fig. 9a and 9b illustrate the concentrator efficiency for the Type I parallel-stacked triangular serrated waveguide medium at different layer counts, while Fig. 9c and 9d present the same for the alternating Type I and Type II triangular serrated waveguide medium.

The simulation results in Fig. 9a show that for the Type I parallel-stacked configuration, when the number of layers is less than or equal to 4, the η initially increases and then decreases as the incident angle increases. However, for layer counts greater than or equal to 5, the concentrator efficiency first rises, then declines, and finally increases again with increasing incident angle. The corresponding overall η avg. for varying stacking layers are shown in Fig. 9b. As the number of layers increases, the overall η avg. initially increases, peaks, and then decreases, with the highest value reached when the layer count is 5. Similarly, Fig. 9c reveals that the trend in η for the alternating Type I and Type II stacked triangular serrated waveguide medium mirrors that shown in Fig. 9a. Fig. 9d demonstrates that the overall η avg. for the alternating stacking configuration also follow a similar pattern to those in Fig. 9b. Notably, for layer counts ranging from 2 to 6, the overall η avg. for the alternating stacking configuration exceed those of the parallel stacking configuration for the same number of layers. In conclusion, the alternating stacking configuration achieves the optimal η avg. when the number of layers is 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integrated application and performance evaluation of FSC with HPSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Building upon the optimized optical waveguide medium structure described above, we integrated it with high-efficiency HPSCs to form a FSC device, as illustrated in Fig. 10 a. The performance of this integrated system was then thoroughly evaluated based on key metrics.

In the fabrication of high-performance FSC devices, we developed high-efficiency fluorescent coatings (serving as fluorescence emission sources) and high-transmittance multilayer optical waveguide structures. Initially, a thin layer of CDs was spin-coated onto the surface of ultra-clear glass substrates using a precise spin-coating technique. By utilizing CDs colloids with varying emission wavelengths, we achieved blue, green, and red fluorescent coatings, as shown in Fig. 10b. Subsequently, optical waveguide medium with Type I and Type II structures were fabricated using PMMA as the base material via injection molding. After the demolding process, the PMMA bulk was cut into five blocks, each of which was polished with sandpaper to create the final Type I and Type II optical waveguide structures with the dimensions of 50 mm × 30 mm × 1.8 mm, as depicted in Fig. 10c.

The fluorescent CDs coating, multilayer optical waveguide medium, and HPSCs were assembled into the FSC device, as shown in Fig. 10d. The inset highlights the physical integration of the multilayer waveguide medium and fluorescent CDs coating, with the effective light-receiving area of the perovskite solar cell measuring 30 mm × 12.5 mm. Photovoltaic performance tests were conducted on perovskite solar cells, both with and without the optical waveguide medium, using a standard solar simulator (AM 1.5G, 100 mW∙cm−2), a J-V and IPCE testing platform. The J-V curves, PCE, and IPCE for the standalone perovskite solar cells were measured under standard illumination conditions. The PCE of the single perovskite cell is 17.31 %, with an average IPCE reaching ∼ 86.95 % at the main absorption wavelength of 400–700 nm, as shown in Fig. S8 and Table 1 .

The optical conversion efficiency (ηopt) is an important parameter for performance evaluation in FSC-PHSC devices, which can be calculated according to the following Equation (3) [28]:
(3)

Similarly, PCE is another essential parameter for assessing the PHSC and the FSC-PHSC were calculated using Equation (5) and (6), respectively [28]:

where JPV , VOC , and FF represent the short-circuit current density, the open-circuit voltage, and the fill factor of PVC (i.e., PHSCs), respectively; JFSC refers to the short-circuit current density of the PVC on the edge of the FSC. Pout represents the output electrical power of the coupled PVC device on the edge of the FSC, normalized by the illuminated area of the PVC, the Pin is the incident optical power density measured by using a power meter directly.

The IPCE of FSC-PHSC was calculated in according to Equation (7) [44]:
(7)
IPCE
( % )

I

where Q in is the photon flux of the monochromic irradiation incident on the window of FSC and Iout is the current through the external circuit. The results of the IPCE analyses were the averages of three measurements.

The J-V curves of the FSCs were measured under AM1.5G simulated solar illumination, as shown in Fig. 10e. Four distinct FSC configurations were tested for comparison. In the first three configurations, sunlight passed through R-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs waveguide media before being absorbed by the high-performance silicon cells (HPSCs), labeled “R-CDs-FSC,” “G-CDs-FSC,” and “B-CDs-FSC,” respectively. The fourth configuration, where sunlight passed through the waveguide medium without fluorescent coatings and was absorbed directly by the HPSCs, is labeled “Blank-FSC.” The key parameters, including short-circuit current density (J SC), open-circuit voltage (V OC), and fill factor (FF), are summarized in Table 1 and Fig. S9. When fluorescent waveguide media were used, the ηopt for R-CDs-FSC, G-CDs-FSC, and B-CDs-FSC were 16.95 %, 15.95 %, and 14.31 %, respectively, representing a significant improvement compared to conventional efficiencies reported in the literature (see Table S2). This enhancement is attributed to the high PLQY, large Stokes shifts, and optimized optical waveguide structures.

The PCEs of the FSC configurations with R-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs coatings were 2.69 %, 2.54 %, and 1.93 %, respectively. In contrast, the Blank-FSC sample, which lacked fluorescent coatings, showed a significant reduction in both ηopt (8.02 %) and PCE (0.64 %), reflecting a twofold decrease in ηopt and a fourfold decrease in PCE. Despite these reductions, the equilateral triangular waveguide structure in the Blank-FSC configuration still allows greater light entry into the solar cell module compared to conventional planar waveguide structures [32,45,46].

To further investigate the changes in photocurrent, we measured the IPCE spectra, as shown in Fig. 10f. For the Blank-FSC configuration, which lacked CD coatings, the IPCE spectrum closely resembled that of the original HPSC. However, in the FSC configurations with B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs coatings, the IPCE spectral intensities were significantly enhanced. Notably, a pronounced photoresponse was observed at the PL peak positions (454 nm for B-CDs, 540 nm for G-CDs, and 656 nm for R-CDs), attributed to the PL generated by CD excitation, which enhances the IPCE at these wavelengths. Interestingly, the IPCE intensity for the R-CDs-FSC was higher than that for the G-CDs-FSC in the 450–800 nm range, despite G-CDs having a higher PLQY, which typically leads to stronger IPCE spectra. This discrepancy is likely due to the larger particle size of R-CDs, which results in enhanced light scattering, further increasing the IPCE [44]. Thus, the IPCE enhancement is closely related not only to PL but also to particle scattering. Additionally, below 420 nm, the IPCE intensity of the B-CDs-FSC was stronger than the others, primarily due to the wavelength conversion effect of B-CDs.

To further assess the stability of the FSCs, we subjected the five sample configurations to continuous illumination, as depicted in Fig. 10g. The stability tests were conducted under controlled environmental conditions (40 ± 5 % humidity and 25 ± 3°C). Over a 20-day period, the normalized PCEs of the HPSCs, R-CDs-FSC, G-CDs-FSC, B-CDs-FSC, and Blank-FSC exhibited decreases of 14.48 %, 2.65 %, 2.73 %, 4.77 %, and 2.54 %, respectively. These results highlight the substantial benefit of integrating optical waveguide media with HPSCs. Not only does this integration reduce the spatial footprint and manufacturing costs of the device, but it also addresses key challenges related to thermal management. By improving heat dissipation, the incorporation of the optical waveguide medium significantly enhances the long-term operational stability of the FSCs, ensuring more reliable and sustained performance under continuous exposure to illumination. Moreover, the relatively lower degradation observed in the FSCs with fluorescent coatings (R-CDs-FSC, G-CDs-FSC, and B-CDs-FSC) compared to the Blank-FSC sample further supports the importance of these coatings in mitigating efficiency loss over time. This stability improvement is crucial for the practical deployment of FSCs in real-world applications, where long-term durability is a key performance criterion.

In conclusion, the study investigates the design and optimization of a FSC using multi-color luminescent CDs integrated with high-efficiency HPSCs. The synthesis of three types of CDs, namely B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, was characterized in terms of their structural, morphological, and optical properties. XRD and TEM analyses confirmed the successful formation of monodisperse spherical nanoparticles with high purity. The PL spectra showed emission across a broad visible spectrum, with each CD type emitting at distinct wavelengths, and the PLQY was evaluated to be highest for the green and red CDs. The study also explores the optimization of the optical waveguide structure for the FSC. Simulation models using COMSOL Multiphysics were used to design optical waveguides that enhanced light-gathering efficiency through total internal reflection. The influence of various optical waveguide medium geometries, including wavy and serrated structures, was assessed, with the triangular-based optical waveguide providing the highest concentration efficiency. The multi-layer waveguide design further improved the light gathering efficiency, with a peak efficiency of 89 % achieved with a five-layer alternating stacking configuration. Finally, the optimized FSC device was fabricated by integrating the fluorescent coatings, waveguide medium, and HPSCs. Performance evaluations demonstrated that while the inclusion of the fluorescent waveguide medium led to a slight decrease in the PCE compared to direct sunlight exposure, it significantly reduced the spatial requirements and cost of the system. Moreover, stability tests showed that the FSC with waveguide medium exhibited improved long-term stability, with reduced performance degradation under continuous illumination. This research highlights the promising potential of using multi-color CDs and optimized waveguide structures for enhancing the efficiency and stability of FSCs, offering a cost-effective approach for future solar energy harvesting technologies.
